item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: F for hamster

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On a crosslinked page, an MC&D employee notes that the pills/their contents eventually moved through the body as is normal for medication. It would have been better if the Foundation had noted something to that effect in their description, of course.

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
A Doomsday entry with dado… a doomsdado if you will.

https://youtu.be/ygxnAtoZ5Vs

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is egregiously unfunny to me, and even with the okay concept, it'd feel so empty and uninteresting, or a harder sell, without the dado dialog, the section dragging this article down the most.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: love dado. how mash plus many more time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dado articles are heralding a new age of glory to the wiki

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sooner or later Dado is going to get his own POI page

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, that's a good point. Thanks for that catch!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is currently unknown exactly how long an individual can survive in this state, although estimates project that, under baseline conditions, given an adequate oxygen and nutrition supply, a human can survive for at least 3,000 years.

This sentence irks me more than dado

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The moment I saw pills, I knew I was going to be enjoying a Dado article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm so sorry if this is a bit pedantic, but those are actually tablets in the picture, not capsules! Capsules are those sort of plastic-y pills.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's like the person who wrote the article is just sick of all the shit the Foundation has to deal with. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it, but at the same time I don't fully get it. Initially the description it puts people in a hibernation-like state, and they could survive for 3000 years, but then it implies that the people buried alive woke up after some double-digit number of years. Normally I'd say something about dado taking sleep too literally, but death isn't possible anyway. Am I missing something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’m really happy someone enjoyed this this as much as the dado stuff. Writing dado was fun, but this was probably my favorite joke of the whole bunch (or it’s at least tied with the hamster one).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: dado’s something that’s hard to do wrong, but also difficult to do well. I like the darker twist this gives his character, yet you still can’t help but chuckle internally. I honestly don’t think dado has to be formulaic; other than him just being goofy, we can dive into some of the shadier shit he’s done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something I don't like about dado is how it could quite easily become formulaic, in the vein of 'poor English skills + medication + pun/wacky misunderstanding/wordplay = article'. Much like a mediocre sitcom character, the appearances of dado don't serve to say something new or interesting, but rather as a set up for a that's-our-dado joke with a laugh track.

I think this skirts the line of that. I didn't downvote because the obvious pun I was expecting about Asian youths fortunately didn't materialise and I liked the tie-in to the doomsday as an amusing and natural way to link something silly to the end of the world and evidence against this being simply application of a formula. I don't think this is a bad article, it just happens to be a possible herald of a trend I wouldn't like to see.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you want more dado, there's at least two or three other articles who use him as a character. I'm horrible at designations so I'm not really helpful telling you about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What?!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The dialogue does it for me. There is something uncomfortable about it. It's not quite a person with a poor grasp of language - but maybe more a "poor grasp of human"; a bit uncanny valley, yeah?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A both deeply depressing and still hilarious interpretation of dado.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it was necessary to determine if the operation was fraudulent or anomalous.

Monitoring of the facility showed that the operation was, to some degree, both fraudulent and anomalous.

Even if not for "hamster still asleep", this is worth an upvote right here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hamster still asleep.

All the dado I could've ever hoped for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First Dado SCP that I've liked,

oh no

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well this is a surprisingly dark way to use Dado. I never could've imagined such an awful (in the good sense) ending coming out of a Dado article, but it works well.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i trust dado

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Haha. Fuck.

First Dado SCP that I've liked, and something that actually made me grimace when it got to the final addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cool SCP entry, the SCP itself isn't super interesting, but the story you've built around it with the mysterious medicine maker and medical facility really hooks you in, and keeps you reading. I like Dado, I hope you use him again, for future SCP entries. +1 for well written SCP.

item.title: More Like Doomsdado, amirite?
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to "Captain Kirby does his best djkaktus impersonation, Part 1 of 1".

Image can be found here: https://www.maxpixel.net/Medicine-Health-Medical-Pills-Medication-Drug-1800347

Thanks to my doomcon team, Stallmantic, Mortos, and Jazstar for crit and feedback.

Shout out to djkaktus for dado.

Dream of amazon prime.

And if you liked this, you can find more of my shenanigans over here!
